content.text: When an AI agent makes a series of LLM calls, invokes tools, reads from memory, and orchestrates sub-agents across async boundaries, a single log line is not enough. You need a structured, causally-linked record of every operation.
OpenTelemetry semantic conventions for GenAI

give every instrumentation library a shared vocabulary — a well-known set of span names and attribute keys — so that traces from a Python orchestrator, a TypeScript tool runner, and a Go memory service can be stitched together and understood by any OTel-compatible backend without per-team translation.
What It Is
The OpenTelemetry GenAI semantic conventions are a vendor-neutral specification under the
opentelemetry-specification
repository that defines the canonical names and types for spans, attributes, and events produced when instrumenting large-language-model calls and agentic AI workflows. The specification lives in the
gen_ai.*

attribute namespace. Its scope covers: LLM chat and completion calls, streaming responses, function/tool calls, token usage, finish reasons, and — through the in-progress Agentic AI extension — orchestrator spans, agent loop iterations, memory reads/writes, and inter-agent delegation.

The conventions are part of OTel's broader semantic convention catalog. Each attribute has a stability level (experimental, stable) and a requirement level (required, recommended, opt-in). Libraries that conform to these conventions produce traces that are understood by any OTel-compatible backend — Jaeger, Tempo, Honeycomb, Datadog, Dynatrace — without custom parsers.
Why It Matters
Agentic systems are distributed systems with an additional layer of non-determinism: the model. Without shared conventions, each team invents their own attribute keys — some use
llm.tokens.input
, others
openai.usage.prompt_tokens
, others
ai.input_token_count

. Dashboards fragment. Alert rules cannot be shared. Vendor integrations must be written separately for every team. The GenAI conventions solve this coordination problem once at the specification level.

For production agentic AI, the stakes are higher than for typical microservices: a single user-visible failure can involve dozens of LLM calls across multiple agents, with the root cause buried in a tool call three iterations into an agent loop. Proper trace hierarchy — enforced by convention — makes this navigable in a trace viewer without custom tooling.
Benefits
Cross-team and cross-service traces are immediately queryable with shared attribute keys — no translation layer required.
Vendor integrations (Langfuse, Phoenix, Datadog GenAI) implement the same convention, so switching backends preserves your dashboards and alerts.
Token usage, cost attribution, and latency percentiles can be computed with standard OTel metrics processors rather than bespoke pipelines.
Propagating W3C trace context through async tool calls links the tool's work back to the originating agent loop without custom correlation IDs.
Auto-instrumentation libraries (opentelemetry-instrumentation-openai, opentelemetry-instrumentation-anthropic) already emit compliant spans, reducing the amount of manual instrumentation needed.
Trade-offs
The agentic AI conventions are still experimental (as of mid-2025). Attribute names may change in a future minor version, requiring a migration across all instrumented services.

Recording prompt and completion content as span events is opt-in for good reason: it creates PII risk and span size blowup at high token counts. Teams must explicitly decide their content-recording policy.

Propagating trace context through async tool calls (e.g., a tool that enqueues a message and returns) requires explicit baggage propagation — the automatic HTTP header injection only covers synchronous HTTP calls.

High-frequency agent loops (dozens of iterations per second) can generate millions of spans per day. Tail-based sampling must be configured carefully to preserve interesting traces without overwhelming the backend.
How It Works

OTel traces are trees of spans. Each span records a unit of work: its name, start/end timestamps, a set of key-value attributes, zero or more events (timestamped log entries within a span), and a status. The GenAI conventions define which span names and attribute keys to use for each type of work in an AI system.
Core gen_ai.* Attributes for LLM Calls
The following attributes are defined on spans whose name is
gen_ai.{operation_name}
gen_ai.chat
or
gen_ai.text_completion
gen_ai.system
— The AI provider: openai, anthropic, google_vertex_ai, aws_bedrock, azure_openai, ollama, etc. (required)
gen_ai.request.model
— Model name as sent in the request: gpt-4o, claude-3-7-sonnet-20250219, etc. (required)
gen_ai.response.model
— Actual model used as returned by the API (may differ from request when using aliases)
gen_ai.usage.input_tokens
— Token count for the input/prompt (required; drives cost attribution)
gen_ai.usage.output_tokens
— Token count for the completion/response (required)
gen_ai.response.finish_reasons
— Array of finish reasons: stop, tool_calls, length, content_filter, error
gen_ai.request.max_tokens
— Max tokens requested (helps diagnose truncated outputs)
gen_ai.request.temperature
— Sampling temperature; useful for correlating output variability with settings
Tool Call Attributes
gen_ai.tool.name
— Name of the function/tool the model requested (required on tool-call spans)
gen_ai.tool.call.id
— Opaque ID assigned by the model to correlate tool call requests with tool results
gen_ai.tool.description
— Human-readable description of what the tool does (opt-in)
Architecture

The diagram below shows the full span hierarchy for a two-iteration agentic request. The trace root represents the inbound user request. Each agent loop iteration is a child span. Within each iteration, LLM calls are children of the loop span. Tool calls and memory reads are children of their parent LLM span.

attrs.alt: OpenTelemetry span hierarchy for a two-iteration agentic AI request showing root, agent loop, LLM call, tool call, and memory read spans

content.text: Span Naming Convention
OTel GenAI span names follow the pattern
{gen_ai.system} {operation_name}
for LLM-level spans (e.g.,
openai chat
{gen_ai.system} {operation_name} {tool_name}
for tool call execution spans, and team-defined names like
agent.loop.iteration
and
agent.memory.retrieve
for orchestrator-level spans (covered by the experimental agentic extension).
When to Use It
Any time an agent makes a call to an LLM provider — whether directly via SDK or through a framework like LangChain, LlamaIndex, or custom orchestration.
When an agent runs a tool (web search, code execution, database query) on behalf of a model's function call.
When building multi-agent pipelines where a parent agent delegates to sub-agents — the trace context must be propagated to link sub-agent spans back to the root.
When you need cross-team trace correlation: the same user request might touch an ingestion service, an orchestration service, and a tool microservice, each owned by a different team.
When you want to publish OTel metrics derived from spans (e.g., token histograms) that feed into cost and latency dashboards without a separate metrics pipeline.
How to Implement
Python: Instrumenting an LLM Call Manually

from opentelemetry import trace
from opentelemetry.sdk.trace import TracerProvider
from opentelemetry.sdk.trace.export import BatchSpanProcessor
from opentelemetry.exporter.otlp.proto.grpc.trace_exporter import OTLPSpanExporter
import openai

# Bootstrap OTel SDK (normally done once at app startup)
provider = TracerProvider()
provider.add_span_processor(BatchSpanProcessor(OTLPSpanExporter()))
trace.set_tracer_provider(provider)

tracer = trace.get_tracer("my-agent", "1.0.0")

def call_llm(messages: list[dict], model: str = "gpt-4o") -> dict:
    """Wrap an OpenAI chat call with OTel GenAI spans."""
    with tracer.start_as_current_span(
        f"openai chat",  # Convention: {gen_ai.system} {operation_name}
        kind=trace.SpanKind.CLIENT,
    ) as span:
        span.set_attribute("gen_ai.system", "openai")
        span.set_attribute("gen_ai.request.model", model)
        span.set_attribute("gen_ai.request.max_tokens", 2048)
        span.set_attribute("gen_ai.request.temperature", 0.2)

        client = openai.OpenAI()
        response = client.chat.completions.create(
            model=model,
            messages=messages,
            max_tokens=2048,
            temperature=0.2,
        )

        # Record usage after the call completes
        usage = response.usage
        span.set_attribute("gen_ai.usage.input_tokens", usage.prompt_tokens)
        span.set_attribute("gen_ai.usage.output_tokens", usage.completion_tokens)
        span.set_attribute("gen_ai.response.model", response.model)
        span.set_attribute(
            "gen_ai.response.finish_reasons",
            [response.choices[0].finish_reason]
        )

        return response
Python: Instrumenting an Agent Loop with Tool Calls

import json
from opentelemetry import trace
from opentelemetry.trace import SpanKind

tracer = trace.get_tracer("agent-orchestrator", "1.0.0")

def run_agent_loop(
    initial_messages: list[dict],
    tools: dict,  # {tool_name: callable}
    agent_id: str,
    max_iterations: int = 10,
) -> str:
    """Run the agent loop, emitting OTel spans at each level."""
    messages = list(initial_messages)

    with tracer.start_as_current_span(
        "agent.request", kind=SpanKind.SERVER
    ) as root_span:
        root_span.set_attribute("agent_id", agent_id)
        root_span.set_attribute("gen_ai.system", "openai")
        root_span.set_attribute("gen_ai.request.model", "gpt-4o")

        for iteration in range(max_iterations):
            with tracer.start_as_current_span(
                "agent.loop.iteration", kind=SpanKind.INTERNAL
            ) as loop_span:
                loop_span.set_attribute("iteration.index", iteration)
                loop_span.set_attribute("agent_id", agent_id)

                # LLM call child span
                with tracer.start_as_current_span(
                    "openai chat", kind=SpanKind.CLIENT
                ) as llm_span:
                    llm_span.set_attribute("gen_ai.system", "openai")
                    llm_span.set_attribute("gen_ai.request.model", "gpt-4o")

                    import openai
                    client = openai.OpenAI()
                    response = client.chat.completions.create(
                        model="gpt-4o",
                        messages=messages,
                        tools=[
                            {"type": "function", "function": {"name": n, "description": ""}}
                            for n in tools
                        ],
                    )
                    usage = response.usage
                    llm_span.set_attribute("gen_ai.usage.input_tokens", usage.prompt_tokens)
                    llm_span.set_attribute("gen_ai.usage.output_tokens", usage.completion_tokens)
                    llm_span.set_attribute(
                        "gen_ai.response.finish_reasons",
                        [response.choices[0].finish_reason]
                    )

                choice = response.choices[0]
                if choice.finish_reason == "stop":
                    return choice.message.content

                # Execute each tool call in its own child span
                tool_results = []
                for tc in choice.message.tool_calls or []:
                    with tracer.start_as_current_span(
                        f"openai chat {tc.function.name}",
                        kind=SpanKind.INTERNAL,
                    ) as tool_span:
                        tool_span.set_attribute("gen_ai.tool.name", tc.function.name)
                        tool_span.set_attribute("gen_ai.tool.call.id", tc.id)
                        tool_span.set_attribute("gen_ai.system", "openai")

                        fn = tools.get(tc.function.name)
                        if fn is None:
                            tool_span.set_status(trace.StatusCode.ERROR, "unknown tool")
                            result = "error: unknown tool"
                        else:
                            args = json.loads(tc.function.arguments)
                            result = fn(**args)

                        tool_results.append({
                            "role": "tool",
                            "tool_call_id": tc.id,
                            "content": str(result),
                        })

                messages.append(choice.message.model_dump())
                messages.extend(tool_results)

        return "max_iterations_reached"
Propagating Trace Context Through Async Tool Calls

When a tool call enqueues work (e.g., publishes to a message queue), the current trace context must be serialized into the message so that the consumer can restore it and create child spans. Use the W3C TraceContext propagator:

from opentelemetry import propagate, trace

# --- Producer side (inside the tool call span) ---
def enqueue_async_work(payload: dict, queue_client) -> None:
    """Inject trace context into message headers before publishing."""
    carrier: dict[str, str] = {}
    propagate.inject(carrier)  # injects traceparent + tracestate headers
    message = {"payload": payload, "otel_carrier": carrier}
    queue_client.publish(message)

# --- Consumer side (in the queue worker) ---
def process_message(message: dict) -> None:
    """Restore trace context from message and create a child span."""
    carrier = message.get("otel_carrier", {})
    ctx = propagate.extract(carrier)  # restores parent span context

    tracer = trace.get_tracer("tool-worker", "1.0.0")
    with tracer.start_as_current_span(
        "tool.async_execution",
        context=ctx,          # makes it a child of the original tool span
        kind=trace.SpanKind.CONSUMER,
    ) as span:
        span.set_attribute("gen_ai.tool.name", "async_research")
        result = do_work(message["payload"])
        span.set_attribute("result.byte_count", len(str(result)))
Examples
YAML Span Attribute Reference
The following YAML documents the attributes that should appear on each span type, with their types and whether they are required:

# Span: "openai chat"  (LLM call span)
attributes:
  gen_ai.system:                  # string, required — "openai" | "anthropic" | ...
  gen_ai.request.model:           # string, required — "gpt-4o"
  gen_ai.response.model:          # string, recommended — actual model used
  gen_ai.usage.input_tokens:      # int, required — prompt token count
  gen_ai.usage.output_tokens:     # int, required — completion token count
  gen_ai.request.max_tokens:      # int, recommended
  gen_ai.request.temperature:     # float, recommended
  gen_ai.request.top_p:           # float, opt-in
  gen_ai.response.finish_reasons: # string[], recommended — ["stop"] | ["tool_calls"]
  gen_ai.response.id:             # string, recommended — provider response ID
  error.type:                     # string, conditionally required on error

---
# Span: "openai chat {tool_name}"  (tool execution span)
attributes:
  gen_ai.tool.name:               # string, required — matches tool's function name
  gen_ai.tool.call.id:            # string, required — ID from the model's tool call
  gen_ai.system:                  # string, required
  gen_ai.tool.description:        # string, opt-in — from tool schema
  error.type:                     # string, conditionally required on error

---
# Span: "agent.loop.iteration"  (orchestrator span — experimental)
attributes:
  agent_id:                       # string, required — stable agent identifier
  iteration.index:                # int, required — 0-based loop counter
  gen_ai.system:                  # string, required
  agent.loop.stop_reason:         # string, on final iteration — "stop" | "max_iter"

---
# Span: "agent.memory.retrieve"  (memory read — experimental)
attributes:
  agent.memory.type:              # string, required — "vector" | "key_value" | "graph"
  agent.memory.query_tokens:      # int, recommended
  agent.memory.results_count:     # int, recommended
Real-World Example

A research assistant agent receives a user question. It runs two agent loop iterations before reaching a final answer. Here is what the trace looks like in an OTel backend such as Jaeger or Honeycomb:
Root span (agent.request): duration 4.2 s, agent_id=research-v2, gen_ai.request.model=gpt-4o
Iteration 1 (agent.loop.iteration, index=0): 2.8 s total duration
LLM call (openai chat): 1.1 s, input_tokens=1240, output_tokens=312, finish_reason=tool_calls
Tool call (openai chat web_search): 1.5 s, tool_name=web_search, call_id=call_abc123, status=success
Memory read (agent.memory.retrieve): 0.2 s, memory_type=vector, results_count=5
Iteration 2 (agent.loop.iteration, index=1): 1.4 s, stop_reason=stop
LLM call (openai chat): 1.4 s, input_tokens=2108, output_tokens=589, finish_reason=stop

Total tokens across the trace: 3,348 input + 901 output. A Prometheus counter derived from these span attributes can feed a Grafana cost dashboard showing dollars per agent run without any bespoke metrics code.
Common Mistakes
Recording token counts only on the root span.
Each LLM call span must carry its own input/output token counts. Aggregating at the root loses per-iteration cost visibility.
Using non-standard attribute names.
Attributes like llm.tokens.in or custom.token_count break compatibility with OTel-native dashboards, alert rules, and auto-instrumentation libraries.
Flattening the span hierarchy.
Creating all spans as siblings under the root instead of nesting them (tool call under LLM call under agent loop) makes it impossible to see which tool call belongs to which LLM call.
Forgetting to propagate context into async tool workers.
If a tool enqueues a job without injecting the trace carrier, the worker's spans become orphan traces with no parent, severing the causal chain.
Recording raw prompt content by default.

The GenAI conventions intentionally make content recording opt-in via span events. Logging prompts in production leaks user PII into trace backends that may not have the same access controls as your primary data store.
Using SpanKind.INTERNAL for all spans.
LLM API calls should use SpanKind.CLIENT. Spans receiving inbound requests should use SpanKind.SERVER. Correct kinds enable automatic request/response latency separation in trace backends.
Best Practices

Use the opentelemetry-instrumentation-openai or opentelemetry-instrumentation-anthropic packages for auto-instrumentation of LLM calls before writing manual spans — they conform to the GenAI conventions and reduce boilerplate.
Set agent_id as a resource attribute (not a span attribute) if every span in a service instance belongs to the same agent. Resource attributes apply to all spans and reduce per-span overhead.
Enable content recording (gen_ai.user.message / gen_ai.assistant.message span events) only in development or for sampled traces designated for debugging — never blanket production.

Derive OTel metrics from span attributes using the OTel Collector's spanmetrics connector, rather than emitting separate metric calls — this keeps token counters and latency histograms automatically consistent with trace data.

Version your instrumentation library alongside your service. When the GenAI spec graduates from experimental, update all instrumented services together using a feature flag or gradual rollout, not piecemeal.
Performance & Scalability
OTel span creation is inexpensive — typically under 1 µs per span for attribute setting, well below the tens-to-hundreds of milliseconds that LLM calls take. The performance cost lies in span export:
BatchSpanProcessor
should always be used in production (never
SimpleSpanProcessor
) because it queues and flushes spans asynchronously, adding no latency to the hot path.
For high-volume deployments, configure tail-based sampling in the OTel Collector. A useful policy for agentic AI: always sample traces containing spans with
error.type
set, always sample traces where total
gen_ai.usage.input_tokens
exceeds 8,000 (expensive requests), and sample 5% of the remainder. This retains all error and high-cost traces for investigation while reducing backend storage costs by 95%.
Tooling & Ecosystem
opentelemetry-instrumentation-openai (Python, traceloop/openllmetry): auto-instruments the OpenAI Python SDK with GenAI-convention spans.
@opentelemetry/instrumentation-openai (Node.js, traceloop/openllmetry-js): same for the OpenAI JS/TS SDK.
Arize Phoenix: local OSS trace viewer with native GenAI convention support, useful for development and evaluation.
Langfuse: OSS/cloud observability platform for LLM applications, supports OTel ingestion on its cloud tier.
Honeycomb, Datadog APM, Dynatrace: all support OTel OTLP ingestion and have GenAI-specific dashboards for gen_ai.* attributes.
OTel Collector spanmetrics connector: derives token count and latency histograms automatically from GenAI spans, eliminating a separate metrics SDK call.
Comparison with Alternatives
Approach
Standardisation
Vendor lock-in
Async propagation
Ecosystem support
OTel GenAI conventions
High — CNCF spec
None
W3C TraceContext propagator
Broad — Datadog, Honeycomb, Grafana, Phoenix
Langfuse SDK (non-OTel)
Low — Langfuse-specific
Langfuse only
Manual — SDK-specific
Langfuse only
Custom structured logs
None
None
Manual correlation ID
Any log backend
Provider-native telemetry (e.g., OpenAI usage API)
Low
Provider only
None
Provider portal only
Security & Reliability
Prompt content in span events.

If you enable the opt-in gen_ai.user.message events, ensure your trace backend applies the same data-access controls as your primary data store. Traces containing user prompts are regulated data in GDPR and HIPAA contexts.
Tool argument redaction.
Tool call arguments (e.g., a SQL query generated by the model) may contain PII. Record tool_name and call_id on spans, but redact argument content unless it has been sanitised.
OTLP exporter authentication.
Always configure OTLP exporters with TLS and bearer token authentication. An unauthenticated OTLP endpoint accepts spans from any source, enabling trace poisoning that can obscure real failures.
Trace context injection attacks.

If you accept trace context from untrusted sources (e.g., inbound HTTP requests from the internet), validate that trace IDs are well-formed and enforce a maximum span nesting depth in your OTel Collector to prevent amplification via deeply nested synthetic traces.
Mental Model
Think of OTel GenAI conventions as a
shared grammar

for describing AI work. Just as a relational database table schema allows any SQL-compatible tool to query its data, a trace that conforms to the GenAI conventions can be queried, visualised, and alerted on by any OTel-compatible tool — no plugin, adapter, or custom parser needed.

The span hierarchy is the causal graph of the agent's work: root request → loop iterations → LLM calls → tool calls. Reading the trace from root to leaf answers "what did the agent do and in what order?". Reading the attribute values at each span answers "what did it cost and did it succeed?". The two questions together give complete operational visibility over the system.
Key Takeaways

The gen_ai.* namespace provides the canonical attribute keys for LLM system, model, token usage, finish reason, and tool call identity — use them exactly as specified to preserve backend compatibility.
The correct span hierarchy is: agent.request > agent.loop.iteration > openai chat > tool call execution. Flattening this hierarchy destroys causal traceability.
Async tool calls require explicit W3C TraceContext propagation (inject at producer, extract at consumer) — HTTP auto-instrumentation does not cover message queues or background workers.
Content recording (prompt/completion text) is intentionally opt-in. Enable it only for designated debug samples, never blanket production, to avoid PII exposure.
Use auto-instrumentation libraries (openllmetry, etc.) first, add manual spans only for orchestrator-level constructs (loop iteration, memory read) that the libraries do not cover.
Tail-based sampling with policies that always retain error traces and high-cost traces keeps trace storage costs manageable without sacrificing debuggability for the cases that matter most.
